md: Multiplying fractions is *easier* than adding them — no common denominator needed. Multiply the numerators, multiply the denominators, simplify.

But *why* does this work? The visualization below shows it as **area**. The unit square is divided into a grid: $\dfrac{n_1}{d_1}$ chooses some columns; $\dfrac{n_2}{d_2}$ chooses some rows. The cells that are in **both** — the intersection — represent the product.

Try it. Set $\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{3}$. The square is split into $2 \times 3 = 6$ cells. One column shaded (half the square), one row shaded (a third). Where they overlap is just **one cell out of six** — that's $\dfrac{1}{6}$.
$\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{1}{6}$. The numerators multiply ($1 \times 1 = 1$), the denominators multiply ($2 \times 3 = 6$).

blocks.title: Multiplying by a whole number
blocks.body: Treat the whole number as a fraction over $1$. So $\dfrac{2}{3} \times 4 = \dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{4}{1} = \dfrac{8}{3}$.

md: **$\dfrac{3}{4} \times \dfrac{2}{9}$.** $\dfrac{6}{36}$. Simplify by dividing top and bottom by $6$: $\dfrac{1}{6}$.

blocks.title: Tip — simplify early
blocks.body: If a numerator and a denominator share a factor, you can cancel it *before* multiplying. $\dfrac{3}{4} \times \dfrac{2}{9}$: cancel a $3$ from top-3 and bottom-9 to get $\dfrac{1}{4} \times \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{2}{12} = \dfrac{1}{6}$. Same answer, smaller numbers along the way.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Multiply the numerators, multiply the denominators, simplify. The 'area' picture explains *why*: the product is the count of overlapping cells in a grid. Easier than addition — no common denominator required.
